questions.stem: A 48-year-old man with hypertension comes for a medication renewal. He plays recreational hockey once a month, has a desk job, and otherwise does not exercise. You ask how many days per week he does moderate-or-greater activity and for how long; he says about 1 day per week for 40 minutes. What is the best interpretation and next step?
options: He does not meet guidelines; advise ~150 min/week of moderate-to-vigorous activity and co-create a realistic SMART goal
He meets guidelines because he completes a bout over 30 minutes
He must have a cardiac stress test before any exercise because he has hypertension
He should focus only on weight loss before attempting exercise
He should start vigorous interval training immediately, since vigorous activity is required for benefit
questions.explain: His PAVS is ~40 min/week, well below the adult target of ~150 min/week plus strengthening ≥2 days/week. Advise and co-create a SMART goal. Stable hypertension is not an absolute contraindication to low/moderate activity, and benefit does not require vigorous intensity.

questions.stem: A 56-year-old woman with LDL-C 4.1 mmol/L wants to try diet before medication and asks which pattern has the strongest LDL-lowering evidence. Which recommendation is most appropriate?
options: Portfolio diet emphasizing nuts, plant protein, viscous fibre, and plant sterols
A high-protein carnivore diet
A generic multivitamin
Iron, B12, and folate supplementation
Avoiding all dietary fat, including nuts and olive oil
questions.explain: The Portfolio diet combines four LDL-lowering components (nuts, plant protein, viscous fibre, plant sterols) and has the strongest LDL-lowering evidence among lifestyle options; it can complement a statin.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old South Asian man has waist circumference 119 cm, triglycerides 2.6 mmol/L, HDL-C 0.4 mmol/L, BP 134/88 mmHg, and fasting glucose 6.2 mmol/L. Which statement is most accurate?
options: He meets metabolic syndrome criteria, having at least 3 of 5 cardiometabolic risk factors
He cannot be classified because his BMI is not provided
Metabolic syndrome requires diagnosed diabetes and a prior myocardial infarction
Waist circumference is not useful because BMI is the only validated adiposity measure
His low HDL-C is protective
questions.explain: Metabolic syndrome needs ≥3 of 5: elevated waist, high triglycerides, low HDL, elevated BP, elevated fasting glucose — he meets all five. BMI is not required, and waist circumference (with ethnicity-specific cutoffs) independently predicts risk.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old patient says, "I know I should exercise more, but I'm not ready. Work is chaotic and I don't know where I'd fit it in." Which stage of change does this best reflect?
options: Contemplation
Precontemplation
Preparation
Action
Maintenance
questions.explain: He recognizes the need to change but is ambivalent and has not committed to action — classic contemplation. The clinical move is to explore pros/cons, values, and confidence.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man with obesity says previous doctors made him feel ashamed, and he now avoids appointments because he expects to be blamed. Which response best reflects patient-centred obesity care?
options: "Would it be okay if we discussed how weight may or may not be affecting your health, and what goals matter most to you?"
"You need to lose weight or you'll keep getting sick."
"Your BMI means you are unhealthy, regardless of labs or function."
"Diet and exercise are simple; it's mainly about willpower."
"We should avoid discussing weight even if you want help."
questions.explain: The best response is permission-based and non-stigmatizing, recognizes BMI alone is insufficient, and centres the patient's goals. The others are stigmatizing, inaccurate, or avoidant.

questions.stem: A 63-year-old woman with osteoarthritis and depression asks whether walking can help, but worries that unless she reaches 150 minutes/week immediately there is no point. Which counselling point is best?
options: The largest health gain often comes from moving from inactivity to even small amounts of activity
Benefits occur only after reaching 150 minutes/week
Physical activity helps only cardiovascular disease, not depression or pain
Resistance training is contraindicated in older adults
Exercise is useful only if it causes weight loss
questions.explain: "Some is better than none" — the biggest gain is moving from no activity to some. Exercise also helps depression, pain/function, CVD and diabetes risk, osteoporosis, and cognition.

questions.stem: A patient asks how to tell whether their exercise intensity is "moderate." Which description is most appropriate?
options: You can talk but not sing
You are unable to speak at all
There is no increase in breathing or heart rate
Your heart rate is above the predicted maximum
You are sweating profusely
questions.explain: Moderate intensity is the "talk but not sing" level; vigorous intensity allows only a few words without pausing.

questions.stem: A sedentary 60-year-old man with stable, treated hypertension wants to begin light-intensity walking. He has no chest pain, syncope, dyspnea, palpitations, or heart-failure symptoms. What is the best advice?
options: Begin low-intensity walking and progress gradually while monitoring symptoms
Avoid all exercise, because hypertension is an absolute contraindication
Start vigorous running immediately to lower blood pressure faster
Exercise only after coronary angiography
Exercise is contraindicated unless BP medications are stopped
questions.explain: A stable patient without red-flag symptoms can usually begin low-intensity walking and progress gradually. Red flags (chest pain, syncope, unexplained dyspnea, decompensated HF, unstable angina) would prompt evaluation first.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old smoker with dyslipidemia and low HDL has a calculated 10-year cardiovascular risk above 20%. Which management principle is most appropriate?
options: High absolute risk supports intensive lifestyle intervention and consideration of statin therapy
Lifestyle counselling is unnecessary once medication is started
Statins are contraindicated in smokers
Framingham includes waist circumference, so no further clinical judgment is needed
LDL-C cannot be modified by diet
questions.explain: High 10-year CVD risk supports both intensive lifestyle change and pharmacologic risk reduction (commonly a statin) — they are complementary, not alternatives. Framingham omits waist circumference and does not replace judgment.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man has unintentionally lost 6% of his body weight over 9 months, with fatigue and decreased appetite. Which statement is most accurate?
options: This is clinically significant and should prompt evaluation for social, psychological, medical, medication-related, and age-related causes
This is normal aging and needs no evaluation
Weight loss in older adults is only concerning if intentional
Obesity is always more dangerous than weight loss in older adults
Pharmacologic appetite stimulation is first-line before any history or exam
questions.explain: Loss of 4–5% in a year (or 10% over any period) is clinically significant in older adults and is usually multifactorial. Evaluate before considering appetite stimulants, which are not first-line.

questions.stem: An 81-year-old woman with unintentional weight loss is examined. Which physical finding is most concerning for serious underlying disease?
options: Lymphadenopathy and hepatosplenomegaly
Mild temporal hollowing alone
Wrinkled skin
Grey hair
A mild reduction in height
questions.explain: Lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, and masses are the worrying findings (malignancy, infiltrative disease). The others are non-specific changes of aging.

questions.stem: A 79-year-old man with poor intake has ill-fitting dentures, social isolation, and recently started a medication that causes nausea. Which management plan is most appropriate?
options: Address dentures, review/adjust the offending medication, assess social supports and food access, involve dietitian/social work, add calorie-dense meals/snacks, and monitor weight
Reassure him that poor intake is expected with aging
Treat only with protein shakes and do not investigate causes
Recommend strict low-fat foods to avoid cardiovascular disease
Start vigorous exercise and delay nutritional intervention
questions.explain: Management targets the reversible causes and barriers: dental issues, offending medications, social supports/food access, dietitian/social-work input, calorie-dense intake, and monitoring. Supplements alone without evaluation miss reversible causes.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman with prediabetes asks which dietary patterns have evidence for improving glycemic control and cardiometabolic risk. Which set is most appropriate?
options: Low-GI, Mediterranean, DASH, and vegetarian/vegan dietary patterns
Paleo, carnivore, Atkins, and grapefruit diets only
Iron/B12/folate supplementation for all patients
A diet defined only by macronutrient percentages with no attention to food quality
A juice cleanse followed by fasting
questions.explain: Low-GI, Mediterranean, DASH, and vegetarian/vegan patterns are evidence-informed for glycemic and cardiometabolic improvement. Patterns that improve multiple risk factors are preferred over single-nutrient tweaks.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old patient has BMI 32 kg/m² but normal BP, normal glucose, excellent function, and no weight-related distress. Which statement best reflects modern obesity assessment?
options: BMI is one screening measure, but diagnosis and treatment planning should consider adiposity-related medical, functional, and psychosocial impairment
BMI alone proves the patient has severe obesity-related illness
BMI is irrelevant and should never be measured
Waist circumference has no relationship to cardiovascular risk
Obesity is an acute disease cured by short-term dieting
questions.explain: BMI is a screening tool, not a diagnosis. Modern assessment (e.g. EOSS) weighs whether adiposity is impairing medical, functional, or psychosocial health — this patient has class I BMI but little impairment.

questions.stem: A patient with obesity has lost weight several times but regained it, and says "I failed again." Which explanation is most accurate and helpful?
options: After weight loss the body can increase hunger and reduce energy expenditure, so long-term support and relapse planning are part of treatment
Weight regain proves you were not motivated enough
There is no biological regulation of body weight
You should stop all activity until your weight stabilizes
Medication is never appropriate for obesity
questions.explain: The hypothalamic set point defends body weight: after loss, hunger rises and energy expenditure falls, promoting regain. This biology reframes obesity as a chronic relapsing disease needing long-term support, not a willpower failure.

questions.stem: A 29-year-old patient with depression wants a non-pharmacologic adjunct. Which physical activity advice is most appropriate?
options: Moderate physical activity can be recommended as part of a broader depression plan, tailored to readiness and preferences
Exercise has no evidence for depression
Only elite athletic training improves mood
Exercise should replace all psychotherapy and medication
Exercise is contraindicated in depression
questions.explain: Exercise is a useful adjunct in depression, tailored to readiness and preferences. It complements rather than replaces indicated psychotherapy or medication.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with a T7 spinal cord injury who uses a wheelchair wants to increase activity. Which question is most important before writing an exercise prescription?
options: "What disability-related symptoms or complications should we consider — skin integrity, bladder routines, autonomic symptoms, pain, fatigue, thermoregulation, or upper-extremity overuse?"
"Can you try harder to walk?"
"Do you agree that wheelchair users cannot exercise effectively?"
"Can your caregiver answer for you?"
"Should we avoid activity because disability makes it unsafe?"
questions.explain: Exercise prescription in disability requires disability-specific safety: skin integrity, bladder/bowel routines, autonomic dysreflexia, pain/fatigue, thermoregulation, and upper-extremity overuse. The principle is adapt, don't avoid.

questions.stem: Which scenario best illustrates the ICF concept of a participation restriction?
options: A person cannot attend a gym because the building has no ramp or accessible equipment
A spinal cord lesion causing paralysis
Difficulty walking due to leg weakness
Reduced range of motion at the shoulder
Neuropathic pain
questions.explain: Participation restriction is difficulty engaging in life roles due to barriers — e.g. an inaccessible gym. Paralysis is an impairment; difficulty walking is an activity limitation.

questions.stem: A physician assumes a patient with a disability must have a poor quality of life and directs all questions to the patient's caregiver instead of the patient. What does this best illustrate?
options: Ableism
Motivational interviewing
Shared decision-making
Trauma-informed care
Population-level prevention
questions.explain: Assuming poor quality of life and speaking to the caregiver rather than the patient are classic examples of ableism, which harms communication, consent, and preventive care.

questions.stem: A 16-year-old, capable of making his own health decisions, tells you he is gay and sexually active but has not told his parents. What is the best approach?
options: Explain confidentiality and its limits, provide appropriate care, and do not disclose his orientation or sexual-health information without consent unless mandatory reporting applies
Tell his parents immediately because he is a minor
Refuse to discuss sexual health
Document it prominently so all family members can see
Tell him confidentiality never has limits
questions.explain: A capable minor's confidentiality should be respected — including sexual orientation and sexual-health information — except where mandatory reporting or imminent-safety limits apply. Explain those limits clearly and provide affirming care.

cards.prompt: State the Geoffrey Rose prevention principle.
cards.answer: Most cases of disease arise among the many people at average/moderate risk, not the few at highest risk — so a small population-wide shift in risk can prevent more total disease than treating only the high-risk tail.

cards.prompt: What are the four components of clinical preventive care?
cards.answer: Counselling, screening, immunization, and chemoprophylaxis — supported by office systems like reminders, tracking, prompts, handouts, and preventive prescriptions.

cards.prompt: How should relapse be framed in behaviour change?
cards.answer: As a normal part of the change process, not a failure — normalize it, learn what got in the way, and re-engage without shame.

cards.prompt: Why is obesity defined as a chronic relapsing disease rather than by BMI alone?
cards.answer: It is adiposity that impairs health and well-being; the body defends its weight via a hypothalamic set point (hunger rises and energy expenditure falls after loss), so it relapses and needs long-term, non-stigmatizing care rather than short-term willpower.

cards.prompt: What makes a dietary pattern the 'best' one for a patient?
cards.answer: That it is evidence-informed AND sustainable for them — fitting their values, culture, budget, cooking skills, food access, and medical needs. Fit drives long-term success more than any single 'optimal' diet.

cards.prompt: Name key risk factors the Framingham score does NOT directly include.
cards.answer: BMI/waist circumference, ethnicity, sleep apnea, family history, and social determinants — so calculators guide but do not replace clinical judgment.

cards.prompt: How is the Physical Activity Vital Sign (PAVS) calculated?
cards.answer: Days per week of moderate-or-greater activity × minutes per day. E.g. 5 days × 30 min = 150 min/week, the adult target.

cards.prompt: Define the three ICF dimensions with an example each.
cards.answer: Impairment = a body structure/function problem (paralysis); activity limitation = difficulty executing a task (walking/transferring); participation restriction = difficulty in life roles due to barriers (an inaccessible gym).

cards.prompt: What is ableism, and give two clinical examples.
cards.answer: A value system that treats certain body/mind characteristics as essential for a worthwhile life and devalues those that function differently. Examples: assuming disability means poor quality of life; speaking to the caregiver instead of the patient.

cards.prompt: What counts as clinically significant unintentional weight loss in an older adult?
cards.answer: ≥4–5% of body weight within 1 year, or ≥10% over any period — associated with frailty, disability, fractures, and mortality, especially at low baseline BMI.

cards.text: Canadian adults should get at least {{150}} minutes/week of moderate-to-vigorous aerobic activity plus muscle/bone strengthening at least {{2}} days/week.
cards.explain: The two-part guideline: aerobic dose plus strengthening.

cards.text: Moderate intensity means you can {{talk}} but not {{sing}}; at vigorous intensity you can say only a few words without pausing.
cards.explain: The talk-test is a practical intensity cue.

cards.text: A FITT exercise prescription specifies Frequency, {{Intensity}}, Time, and {{Type}}.
cards.explain: FITT structures the written exercise prescription.

cards.text: Metabolic syndrome generally requires at least {{3}} of 5: elevated waist, high triglycerides, low {{HDL}}, elevated blood pressure, and elevated fasting glucose.
cards.explain: BMI is not one of the five and is not required.

cards.text: A SMART goal is Specific, Measurable, {{Achievable}}, Relevant, and {{Time-bound}}.
cards.explain: Concrete SMART goals outperform vague intentions like 'be healthier'.

cards.text: The Portfolio diet combines nuts, plant protein, viscous {{fibre}}, and plant {{sterols}} to lower LDL cholesterol.
cards.explain: Four food components with complementary LDL-lowering mechanisms.

cards.text: Use person-first language: a {{person living with obesity}}, not an 'obese patient', to reduce weight {{stigma}}.
cards.explain: Weight stigma drives care avoidance and worse outcomes.

cards.text: In older-adult weight loss, the most concerning exam findings are {{lymphadenopathy}}, hepatosplenomegaly, and {{masses}}.
cards.explain: These point to malignancy or infiltrative disease.

cards.prompt: Which is a population-level (not high-risk individual) prevention strategy?
options: Sodium-reduction policy and smoke-free public spaces
Prescribing a statin to a high-risk patient
Treating one patient's hypertension
Referring a patient to a dietitian
cards.explain: Population strategies shift the whole risk curve (sodium policy, smoke-free spaces); the others target individuals.

cards.prompt: Which statement best reflects the motivational-interviewing principle of autonomy?
options: "It's ultimately your decision; we can choose what feels realistic."
"You must start exercising today or you'll get sicker."
"I'll decide the plan and you follow it."
"There's no point discussing it if you won't commit."
cards.explain: Autonomy support affirms the patient's choice and control, which evokes their own motivation.

cards.prompt: Why can BMI misclassify a muscular athlete?
options: BMI does not distinguish fat mass from muscle/bone mass
BMI overweights height
BMI measures fat distribution directly
BMI includes waist circumference
cards.explain: BMI is weight/height² and cannot separate fat from lean mass or show fat distribution, so it misclassifies athletes, sarcopenic older adults, and others.

cards.prompt: Which is a recognized adverse effect/caution of statins?
options: Myalgias and rare rhabdomyolysis; avoid in pregnancy
Hyperkalemia
Pulmonary fibrosis
Agranulocytosis
cards.explain: Statins can cause myalgias, rare rhabdomyolysis, LFT elevation, and a small diabetes signal; they are avoided in pregnancy and active liver disease.

cards.prompt: Which symptom should prompt medical evaluation before moderate/vigorous exercise?
options: Exertional chest pain or syncope
Mild post-exercise muscle soreness
Feeling slightly out of breath on a brisk walk
Improved sleep after activity
cards.explain: Chest pain, syncope/presyncope, unexplained dyspnea, palpitations, or heart-failure symptoms are red flags requiring evaluation; mild soreness and breathlessness on exertion are expected.

cards.prompt: Which obesity intervention is the most effective for durable weight loss and improving diabetes/CVD risk in severe obesity?
options: Bariatric surgery
Orlistat
A short-term low-fat diet
A daily multivitamin
cards.explain: Bariatric surgery is the most effective durable intervention for selected patients with severe obesity, though it requires lifelong follow-up and carries surgical/nutritional risks.

cards.prompt: What is the appropriate stance on conversion therapy?
options: It is rejected by major professional organizations and is harmful; affirming care is the standard
It is an evidence-based counselling option
It is appropriate for transgender patients only
It should precede any confidentiality discussion
cards.explain: Conversion therapy is rejected by major professional bodies and is associated with harm; affirming, patient-centred care is the standard.

cards.prompt: Why are sugar-sweetened beverages a high-yield dietary target?
options: They add calories with poor satiety and worsen cardiometabolic risk
They are the only source of dietary sugar
They improve HDL cholesterol
They reduce blood pressure
cards.explain: SSBs deliver calories without satiety and worsen cardiometabolic risk, making them an efficient target.

cards.prompt: An inaccessible gym with no adapted equipment is which type of barrier to activity?
options: Institutional
Intrapersonal
Interpersonal
Genetic
cards.explain: Inaccessible facilities and untrained staff are institutional barriers; pain/fatigue are intrapersonal, and transport/caregiver needs are interpersonal.

cards.prompt: Which are among the five metabolic syndrome criteria? (select all)
options: Elevated waist circumference
Low HDL cholesterol
Elevated fasting glucose
Elevated BMI
cards.explain: The five are elevated waist, high triglycerides, low HDL, elevated BP, and elevated fasting glucose. BMI is not one of them.

cards.prompt: Regular exercise reduces the risk of which conditions? (select all)
options: Type 2 diabetes
Depression
Colon cancer
Acute appendicitis
cards.explain: Exercise lowers risk of diabetes, depression/anxiety, several cancers (breast, colon), CVD, stroke, osteoporosis, and dementia. Appendicitis is unrelated.

cards.prompt: Which are recognized categories in the differential for older-adult weight loss? (select all)
options: Social (isolation, food insecurity)
Psychological (depression, dementia)
Medical (cancer, hyperthyroidism)
An obligatory consequence of normal aging
cards.explain: The four buckets are social, psychological, medical, and age-related/medication. Significant loss is not an obligatory part of normal aging.

cards.prompt: Which are high-yield LGBTQ2S confidentiality scenarios? (select all)
options: A youth not out to their parents
A trans patient using different names in different settings
An older adult fearing discrimination in long-term care
A routine blood-pressure check with no identity concerns
cards.explain: Youth, trans patients across settings, and older adults in care are classic confidentiality flashpoints; ask preferences and avoid non-consensual disclosure.

cards.prompt: Which approaches should be AVOIDED in lifestyle counselling? (select all)
options: Blame, shame, and lecturing
Weight as the only success marker
Ignoring financial and access barriers
Asking permission to discuss a topic
cards.explain: Avoid blame/shame, weight-centric-only goals, and ignoring barriers; asking permission is a recommended, non-stigmatizing practice.

cards.prompt: Order the 5A framework for exercise counselling:
cards.explain: Ask → Assess → Advise → Agree → Assist.

cards.prompt: Order the patient-centred obesity (5A) approach:
cards.explain: The obesity 5As mirror the counselling 5As, beginning with permission.

cards.prompt: Order a sensible evaluation of older-adult unintentional weight loss:
items: Confirm and quantify the weight loss
Review intake, swallowing, dental, mood/cognition, and medications
Examine for nodes, masses, and hepatosplenomegaly
Basic labs and targeted investigations, then treat causes
cards.explain: Confirm → history/med review → focused exam → labs and cause-directed treatment.

cards.prompt: Order the transtheoretical stages of change:
cards.explain: Precontemplation → contemplation → preparation → action → maintenance (with relapse possible at any point).

cards.prompt: Match the dietary pattern to its best-known use:
cards.explain: Patterns are chosen for the target risk factor and patient fit.

cards.prompt: Match the stage of change to its clinical move:
cards.explain: Match the intervention to the patient's readiness.

cards.prompt: Match the ICF dimension to its example:
pairs: Cannot use a gym with no ramp
cards.explain: Impairment → body; activity limitation → task; participation restriction → life role/barrier.

cards.prompt: Match the lipid-lowering agent to its mechanism/role:
pairs: First-line LDL lowering; cuts CV events
cards.explain: Statin first-line; ezetimibe add-on; PCSK9 inhibitor for selected very-high-risk patients.

cards.prompt: Match the term to its definition:
cards.explain: Distinguish general movement, structured exercise, sedentary time, and the fitness gold standard.

cards.prompt: Match the barrier level to an example:
pairs: Need for transport or a support person
cards.explain: Barriers operate at multiple levels and each needs different solutions.

cards.prompt: Why is waist circumference useful alongside BMI, and why are cutoffs ethnicity-specific?
cards.answer: Central adiposity independently predicts cardiometabolic risk that BMI misses, and risk thresholds differ by ethnicity (e.g. lower waist cutoffs signal increased risk in South Asian patients).

cards.prompt: What happens to systolic and diastolic BP during dynamic aerobic exercise?
cards.answer: Systolic BP rises with workload while diastolic BP stays similar or slightly decreases; cardiac output and heart rate rise, and stroke volume increases then plateaus.

cards.prompt: What are the general principles for anti-obesity pharmacotherapy?
cards.answer: Use when benefits outweigh risks, typically BMI ≥30 (or ≥27 with a complication), alongside lifestyle support; GLP-1 agonists reduce appetite (GI effects), orlistat reduces fat absorption; avoid in pregnancy; therapy is often chronic because obesity is chronic and relapsing.

cards.prompt: What unique needs do LGBTQ2S older adults have in care settings?
cards.answer: Lifelong stigma exposure, fear of re-closeting in long-term care, reliance on chosen family (sometimes lower family-of-origin support), higher isolation risk, and a need for inclusive advance-care planning and protected confidentiality.

cards.prompt: What are the main components of energy expenditure?
cards.answer: Basal metabolic rate, physical activity/exercise, non-exercise activity thermogenesis (daily activities), the thermic effect of food, and adaptive thermogenesis — several of which fall after weight loss, promoting regain.

guides.title: Week 17 · 1 Health Promotion & Prevention
guides.html:
Big picture

Health Promotion & Prevention

Week 17 · Health Promotion
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

Lifestyle drives non-communicable disease — and why shifting a whole population can beat chasing only the high-risk few.

Lifestyle medicine

Lifestyle behaviours are major drivers of non-communicable disease (NCD): hypertension, type 2 diabetes, dyslipidemia, cardiovascular disease, obesity complications, depression, osteoporosis, dementia, and some cancers. The clinical task is not to "tell patients what to do" but to help them make sustainable changes that fit their goals, readiness, culture, finances, and access.

ConditionKey lifestyle lever
HypertensionDASH, sodium reduction, aerobic + resistance exercise, alcohol/smoking
DyslipidemiaPortfolio/Mediterranean pattern, unsaturated fats; statin when indicated
Type 2 diabetesActivity, weight management, low-GI/Mediterranean/DASH patterns
Depression / dementiaExercise as adjunct/prevention

Individual vs population prevention

ApproachStrengthLimit
High-risk individualTargets those most likely to benefit; personalizedMisses the many cases arising from average-risk people
Population-levelShifts the whole risk curve; may prevent more total casesSmall effect per person; political/autonomy challenges
Geoffrey Rose principle: most cases arise among people at average/moderate risk (because there are so many of them), so a small population-wide shift can prevent more disease than treating only the high-risk tail.

Clinical prevention tools

Preventive care = counselling, screening, immunization, and chemoprophylaxis. Office systems that help: chart and patient reminders, tracking systems, visual prompts, handouts, patient-held preventive records, and "preventive prescriptions."

Population examples
Sodium-reduction policy, smoke-free public spaces, sugar-sweetened-beverage measures, and active-transportation infrastructure all shift average risk without requiring each individual to act.

guides.title: Week 17 · 2 Behaviour Change & Communication
guides.html:
Counselling

Behaviour Change & Communication

Week 17 · Health Promotion
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

Information alone rarely changes behaviour — motivational interviewing, the stages of change, and SMART goals do the work.

Why "just educate" fails

Patients rarely change from information alone. Sustainable change is likelier when they feel understood, respected, and empowered, with a plan tailored to readiness, values, barriers, and supports. Use person-first, non-stigmatizing language and permission-based openers ("Would it be okay if we talked about your activity today?").

Motivational interviewing (MI)

A collaborative style that supports autonomy and evokes the patient's own reasons for change.

ElementIn practice
CollaborationWork with, not over, the patient
EvocationDraw out their own motivations
Autonomy"It's ultimately your decision"
Reflective listening"You want to be active, but time and fatigue get in the way"
Open questions + affirmationsInvite exploration; recognize strengths

Transtheoretical stages of change

StageClinical move
Precontemplation (no intent)Build rapport, ask permission, raise awareness gently
Contemplation (ambivalent)Explore pros/cons, values, confidence
Preparation (intends soon)SMART goals, supports, troubleshoot barriers
ActionReinforce, problem-solve setbacks
MaintenanceRelapse prevention, revise goals
Relapse is part of the change process, not failure — normalize it, learn from it, re-engage without shame.

SMART goals

Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound. Good: "Walk briskly 15 min after dinner on Mon/Wed/Fri for 2 weeks." Weak: "Lose weight and be healthier."

guides.title: Week 17 · 3 Obesity & Weight Management
guides.html:
Chronic disease

Obesity & Weight Management

Week 17 · Health Promotion
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

A chronic relapsing disease of adiposity-related impairment — diagnosed beyond BMI, managed long-term, free of stigma.

Definition & measurement

Obesity is a chronic, progressive, relapsing disease of adiposity that impairs health and well-being — not defined by BMI alone. Diagnosis weighs functional, medical, and psychosocial impairment.

BMICategory
18.5–24.9Normal range
25.0–29.9Overweight
30.0–34.9 / 35–39.9 / ≥40Obesity class I / II / III

BMI limits: ignores fat vs muscle/bone, ignores fat distribution, and misclassifies athletes, sarcopenic older adults, and pregnancy. Waist circumference adds value (central adiposity predicts cardiometabolic risk); cutoffs are ethnicity-specific (e.g. lower thresholds for South Asian patients).

Edmonton Obesity Staging System (EOSS)
EOSS grades severity by health impact (metabolic, physical, psychological, functional) rather than BMI, and predicts morbidity/mortality better than BMI alone.

Weight bias, set point & regain

Weight stigma harms health — it drives care avoidance, stress, disordered eating, and less activity. Use person-first language ("person living with obesity"). The body defends its weight: after loss, hunger rises and energy expenditure falls (hypothalamic set point), promoting regain — which is why care is long-term, not a willpower test.

The 5A approach & options

Ask permission → Assess drivers/complications/goals → Advise (non-stigmatizing) → Agree realistic goals → Assist with resources/referrals.

OptionNote
Behavioural/nutrition/activity/psychologicalFoundation; benefit even with modest weight loss
PharmacotherapyBMI ≥30 (or ≥27 + complication); GLP-1 agonists ↓appetite (GI effects); orlistat ↓fat absorption
Bariatric surgeryMost durable; improves diabetes/CVD risk; needs lifelong follow-up

guides.title: Week 17 · 4 Nutrition & Medical Nutrition Therapy
guides.html:
Diet

Nutrition & Medical Nutrition Therapy

Week 17 · Health Promotion
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

There is no single best diet — pick a sustainable, evidence-informed pattern, and know the Portfolio, DASH, and Mediterranean stars.

Patterns, not single nutrients

The best diet is the one that is evidence-informed and sustainable for that patient — fit with values, culture, budget, skills, and food access drives long-term success. Modern counselling emphasizes whole-food dietary patterns over "eat less fat / fewer carbs."

PatternBest-known use
PortfolioLDL lowering (highest-yield lifestyle change for LDL)
DASHHypertension / CVD risk
MediterraneanCVD benefit; practical for adherence
Low-GI, vegetarian/veganGlycemic + cardiometabolic improvement

The Portfolio diet

An evidence-based cholesterol-lowering pattern combining four LDL-lowering components:

ComponentTargetMechanism
Nuts~45 g/dayUnsaturated fat, fibre, plant protein
Plant protein~50 g/daySoy, legumes, tofu replace animal protein
Viscous fibre~20 g/dayOats, barley, psyllium — bind bile acids
Plant sterols~2 g/dayReduce intestinal cholesterol absorption
Portfolio is the highest-yield lifestyle change for LDL reduction and can be combined with a statin. Refer to a dietitian when possible.

The sat-fat / sugar / sodium nuance

Avoid "one nutrient is everything" messaging. Replacement matters: swapping saturated fat for unsaturated fat or whole-food carbs helps; swapping it for refined starch/sugar does not. Sugar-sweetened beverages are high-yield targets (calories with poor satiety). Sodium reduction helps BP, especially salt-sensitive hypertension, but overall food quality still matters.

guides.title: Week 17 · 5 CV Risk, Metabolic Syndrome & Lipids
guides.html:
Cardiometabolic

CV Risk, Metabolic Syndrome & Lipids

Week 17 · Health Promotion
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

Cluster the risk factors, estimate 10-year risk, and layer statins on top of lifestyle — not instead of it.

Metabolic syndrome

A cluster that raises risk of CVD, type 2 diabetes, and mortality. Diagnosis generally needs ≥3 of 5:

  • Elevated waist circumference
  • Elevated triglycerides
  • Low HDL-C
  • Elevated blood pressure
  • Elevated fasting glucose

Each component is independently risky, and risk rises as they accumulate. BMI is not required for the diagnosis.

Cardiovascular risk assessment

Inputs: age, sex, BP, smoking, diabetes, total/HDL/LDL cholesterol (or non-HDL/ApoB), family history, CKD, ethnicity/social determinants. In Canada the Framingham Risk Score estimates 10-year risk from age, sex, total and HDL cholesterol, systolic BP (and treatment), smoking, and diabetes.

Framingham limits
It does not directly include BMI/waist, ethnicity, sleep apnea, family history, or social determinants. Calculators guide but do not replace clinical judgment.

Lipid pharmacotherapy

AgentRole / note
StatinFirst-line LDL lowering; cuts events. AEs: myalgia, rare rhabdomyolysis, LFT rise, small diabetes signal; avoid in pregnancy/active liver disease
Ezetimibe↓intestinal cholesterol absorption; add-on or statin intolerance
PCSK9 inhibitorInjectable; very-high-risk or familial hypercholesterolemia; effective but costly
A high-risk patient often needs both lifestyle change and a statin — offering a statin does not mean lifestyle is unimportant. They are complementary.

guides.title: Week 17 · 6 Physical Activity & Exercise Prescription
guides.html:
Exercise

Physical Activity & Exercise Prescription

Week 17 · Health Promotion
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

Some is better than none: the guidelines, the PAVS, and writing a FITT prescription — plus the red flags that need a check first.

Definitions & benefits

TermMeaning
Physical activityAny movement increasing energy expenditure
ExercisePlanned, structured, repetitive activity for fitness
Cardiorespiratory fitnessO₂ delivery/use; VO₂ max is the gold standard
Sedentary behaviourLow-expenditure waking sitting/reclining

Exercise lowers risk of all-cause mortality, CVD, hypertension, type 2 diabetes, stroke, breast/colon cancer, depression/anxiety, osteoporosis, and dementia.

"Some is better than none" — the biggest gain is when a sedentary person becomes even modestly active.

Canadian adult guideline (18–64)

≥150 min/week moderate-to-vigorous aerobic activity, plus muscle/bone strengthening ≥2 days/week. Intensity cue: moderate = can talk but not sing; vigorous = only a few words without pausing.

Acute & chronic physiology

Acute: cardiac output and HR rise (HR ~linear with workload), stroke volume rises then plateaus, systolic BP rises (diastolic stable/slightly down in dynamic aerobic), ventilation increases. Training: lower resting HR, higher stroke volume/VO₂ max, better BP, insulin sensitivity, mood, sleep, lipids; resistance training builds muscle and bone.

PAVS, 5A & FITT

Physical Activity Vital Sign: days/week × minutes/day (e.g. 5 × 30 = 150 min/wk). Counsel with the 5As (Ask, Assess, Advise, Agree, Assist) and write a FITT prescription: Frequency, Intensity, Time, Type.

Evaluate before moderate/vigorous exercise if
Chest pain/pressure, unexplained dyspnea, syncope/presyncope, palpitations/arrhythmia, orthopnea/PND, heart-failure edema, symptomatic murmur, claudication, or recent ACS/unstable angina/decompensated HF. Low-intensity walking is usually safe for most patients.

guides.title: Week 17 · 7 Disability, Exercise Adaptation & Ableism
guides.html:
Equity

Disability, Exercise Adaptation & Ableism

Week 17 · Health Promotion
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

Adapt activity, don't avoid it — the ICF model, disability-specific safety, and how ableism harms care.

The ICF model

DimensionExample
Impairment (body structure/function)Paralysis after spinal cord injury
Activity limitation (executing a task)Difficulty walking/transferring
Participation restriction (life roles)Can't use a gym with no ramp/accessible equipment

Adults with disabilities have higher inactivity and higher risk of heart disease, stroke, diabetes, and cancer — and a physician recommendation substantially increases participation.

Barriers & adapted 5As

Barriers span intrapersonal (pain, fatigue, spasticity, fear), interpersonal (transport, caregiver time), institutional (inaccessible gyms, untrained staff), and community/policy (cost, transit, funding). Apply the 5As, adding disability-specific safety in the Ask step.

Example — T7 spinal cord injury
Consider wheelchair-accessible options, upper-extremity overuse, skin breakdown, autonomic dysreflexia, neurogenic bladder/bowel schedule, thermoregulation, and cardiovascular risk. Any increase helps — adapt, don't avoid.

Ableism

Ableism treats certain body/mind characteristics as essential for a valuable life and devalues those that function differently. Common assumptions: disability = poor quality of life; the goal is always cure; speaking to the caregiver instead of the patient; ignoring accessibility; infantilizing language.

Consequences & the fix
Ableism causes missed diagnoses, poor consent, care avoidance, and worse preventive care. Instead: ask what accommodations are needed, address the patient directly, ensure accessible spaces/timing, recognize the patient's expertise about their own body, and avoid quality-of-life assumptions.

guides.title: Week 17 · 8 LGBTQ2S Health & Older-Adult Weight Loss
guides.html:
Equity + geriatrics

LGBTQ2S Health & Older-Adult Weight Loss

Week 17 · Health Promotion
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

Confidentiality and affirming care for LGBTQ2S patients, and the multifactorial workup of unintentional weight loss in older adults.

LGBTQ2S health & confidentiality

LGBTQ2S communities have faced medical, legal, and social discrimination (homosexuality and trans identity were historically pathologized; conversion therapy caused harm), fuelling mistrust and care avoidance. Build safer environments proactively.

High-yield confidentiality scenarios
Youth not out to parents; trans patients using different names/pronouns in different settings; HIV-status disclosure; older adults in long-term care. Ask what name/pronouns to use in clinic, phone, mail, referrals, and with family; do not disclose orientation, gender identity, trans status, or HIV status without consent unless legally required. For a capable minor, respect confidential care except where mandatory reporting applies.

Conversion therapy is rejected by major professional organizations and is harmful — affirming, patient-centred care is the standard. LGBTQ2S older adults may fear re-closeting in care, rely on chosen family, and need inclusive advance-care planning.

Unintentional weight loss in older adults

Significant: ≥4–5% in a year or ≥10% over any period — associated with frailty, disability, fractures, and mortality, especially at low baseline BMI.

CategoryExamples
SocialIsolation, poverty, can't shop/cook, food insecurity
PsychologicalDepression, dementia, bereavement
MedicalCancer, infection, hyperthyroidism, malabsorption, dysphagia
Age-related / medsAnorexia of aging, taste/smell, polypharmacy (SSRIs, metformin, digoxin, opioids)
Workup & management
Confirm and quantify loss; review intake, swallowing, dental, mood/cognition, social supports; exam for lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, masses; basic labs (CBC, electrolytes, LFTs, TSH, glucose). Treat the cause, deprescribe offenders, involve dietitian/social work, use calorie-dense meals/snacks; appetite-stimulant drugs are not first-line. Reassess in ~3 months.

guides.title: Week 17 · 9 Cram Sheet
guides.html:
Night-Before High-Yield

Health Promotion

Week 17 · Health Promotion
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite
★ Do-not-miss · first-step reflexes
Pre-exercise red flags (chest pain, syncope, unexplained dyspnea, palpitations, HF signs) → evaluate before moderate/vigorous exercise
Older-adult unintentional weight loss ≥4–5%/yr or ≥10% over any period → evaluate (don't dismiss as aging)
Weight-loss exam red flags → lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, masses
High 10-yr CV risk → intensive lifestyle and statin (complementary, not either/or)
Capable minor (sexual orientation / sexual health) → don't disclose without consent unless mandatory reporting applies
Anti-obesity / GLP-1 / statin → avoid in pregnancy

Health promotion Prevention

Geoffrey Rose most cases arise at average risk → population shift beats chasing the tail
Two approaches high-risk individual vs population-level
Prevention counselling, screening, immunization, chemoprophylaxis
Population levers sodium policy, smoke-free spaces, SSB measures, active transport
Lifestyle drives HTN, T2DM, dyslipidemia, CVD, several cancers

Behaviour change Counselling

Motivational interviewing collaboration, evocation, autonomy, reflective listening
Stages precontemplation → contemplation → preparation → action → maintenance
Relapse part of the process — normalize, re-engage
SMART Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound
Approach person-first, non-stigmatizing, permission-based
Avoid blame/shame, lecturing, weight-only goals

Obesity Weight

Disease chronic, relapsing; NOT BMI alone — EOSS grades by health impact
Set point after loss: hunger ↑, expenditure ↓ → regain (not willpower)
5A Ask permission, Assess, Advise, Agree, Assist
Pharmacotherapy BMI ≥30 (or ≥27 + complication); bariatric = most durable
Language person-first; weight stigma harms care

Nutrition Diet

Portfolio highest-yield for LDL (nuts, plant protein, viscous fibre, sterols)
DASH / Mediterranean BP / CVD risk
Low-GI, veg patterns glycemic control
Best diet the sustainable, evidence-informed one
Replacement matters sat fat → unsaturated (not refined starch/sugar)
High-yield target sugar-sweetened beverages

CV risk & lipids Cardiometabolic

Metabolic syndrome ≥3 of 5: waist, TG, low HDL, BP, fasting glucose
Framingham 10-yr risk; omits BMI/waist, ethnicity, family history
Statin first-line LDL lowering; cuts events
Ezetimibe / PCSK9 add-on / intolerance · very-high-risk or familial hypercholesterolemia
Key lifestyle + statin are complementary

Exercise prescription Exercise

Guideline ≥150 min/wk moderate-vigorous + strengthening ≥2 d/wk
Moderate talk but not sing
PAVS days/wk × min/day
Counsel 5A; write a FITT prescription (Frequency, Intensity, Time, Type)
Dose some is better than none

Disability & ableism Equity

ICF impairment · activity limitation · participation restriction (e.g. inaccessible gym)
Principle adapt, don't avoid
SCI safety skin integrity, autonomic dysreflexia, bladder/bowel, overuse
Barriers intrapersonal / interpersonal / institutional / community
Ableism assuming poor QoL; speaking to the caregiver, not the patient

LGBTQ2S & older-adult wt loss Equity + Geri

Confidentiality capable minor; trans names/pronouns; no non-consensual disclosure
Conversion therapy rejected and harmful → affirming care
Older-adult wt loss ≥4–5%/yr or ≥10% = significant
Causes social / psychological / medical / age-medication buckets
Exam red flags lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, masses
Appetite stimulants NOT first-line — evaluate the cause first
Week 17 · Health Promotion · cram sheet · cue → answer

facets.use: First-line LDL lowering / CVD risk reduction
facets.adverse: Myalgia, rare rhabdomyolysis, LFT rise, small diabetes signal
facets.contra: Pregnancy, active liver disease; drug interactions

facets.use: Add-on when LDL target unmet / statin intolerance

facets.mechanism: GLP-1 receptor agonist — decreases appetite, improves glycemia
facets.contra: Avoid in pregnancy; certain endocrine cancer syndromes (agent label)

facets.mechanism: Pancreatic lipase inhibitor — reduces fat absorption
facets.adverse: Oily stools, GI symptoms, fat-soluble vitamin malabsorption

facets.mechanism: Partial α4β2 nicotinic agonist (reduces cravings + reward)

facets.use: Smoking cessation (also reduces post-cessation weight gain)
Type 2 diabetes; prediabetes / metabolic syndrome risk reduction
facets.mechanism: Biguanide — decreases hepatic gluconeogenesis, improves insulin sensitivity
facets.adverse: GI upset, B12 deficiency; rare lactic acidosis
facets.contra: Hold with advanced renal impairment / contrast
